sentences.text: They were all from Belgium.
They were all from Belgium.
Oh, yes.
Not at that time.
As far as I know, I was the first.
We were-- my brother and I were the first.
I was not aware.
But then, I might not have known.
I mean, out of 350 children, I certainly, you know, was never given the opportunity to go and look.
No, nothing, nothing.
All they told me that it probably wouldn't be for very long, and that we'd all be together again in the near future, and that's all.
I mean, they hadn't a clue where I would be.
Not at the time.
It-- and the war hadn't broken out yet.
Belgium was a neutral country.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, was not-- I mean, I was lucky I went to Belgium.
Well, it made no difference, obviously, the neutrality was not observed.
But children went to France or Holland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It-- it was not dangerous at that time.
The Jewish children were not being arrested until, I think, you know, the early '40s, men were arrested, not women, or no children.
Until '42 actually, I-- I-- I-- until 1942, it was basically men who were being arrested.
We were sent away as a precautionary measure-- measure at the time.
I don't remember exactly.
It was maybe November, the 18th or 19th, that I left Germany.
Something like that.
The war was-- the war was-- Poland was still a year away.
Yes.
I saw him.
I tried to see him.
I was allowed to see him about once a day.
But the orphanage soon realized that they could not handle him.
They were not equipped.
They were not-- their mission was not to look after-- while they looked after physically handicapped children, they could not deal with his mental handicap.
It was too severe for them to handle.
I know they had slow learners and children with learning problems.
The orphanage even had a special school for these children.
The only school that the orphanage had was for children.
There must have been quite a few, because it was a large-- to me, it looked like a large building.
But they couldn't handle him, so they sent him away probably within a month of his arrival.
No, no.
I found out just that he wasn't there anymore.
They send him to a special village in Belgium called Geel, G-E-E-L, which is a very unusual place.

The population of this small farming village-- I'm not quite sure what the population is, but it's a few thousand, it's not very large-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it made a pact with a Saint back in the 15th century to take care of special children, perhaps even special adults.
I don't remember.

And, indeed, just about every family in that village had a handicapped-- mentally handicapped, not physically handicapped-- or somebody with some learning disability, or-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he was there for many, many years.
He was with one family.
Of course, I didn't know that at the time.
I learned this afterwards.
I didn't hear from my brother until after the war, really.
He did not write.
I did not write.
I didn't know where he was.
Nobody-- if they told me, it made no difference.
I couldn't-- I could not communicate with him.
He lived with one family for many years and then lived with different families in that village until the late '50s or early '60s, when his behavior changed.
He became violent.
He became-- he started breaking windows or smashing furniture.
I'm not quite sure.
I never got all the details.
And for maybe a few months, he was placed in a psychiatric institution.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In that same village, they had a psychiatric institution.
And I remember, we visited him there.
This was, of course, long after the war.
My late husband and I visited him there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually he was sent to Saint Truiden.
Saint-- Saint Truiden, T-R-U-I-D-E-N, which is another small village in Belgium, which has a very large psychiatric institution.
And here to this day, he's there.
He's been there all his life.
I mean, he's been in Belgium.
Belgians have taken care of him lovingly and beautifully all of his life.
It's incredible.
They have never asked for a penny.
In fact, when my mother passed away, I had made sure that she left him some money.
I didn't think it was fair that, you know, because he was handicapped, that he'd be left out of her will.
And we took the money over there, and my husband and I, on our yearly visit.
And we told them that this was not really for him.
He doesn't need anything.
He's not-- he doesn't-- you know, things mean nothing to him.
He doesn't want a radio.
He doesn't want a watch.
He got-- he has all the clothes he needs.
He has pocket money, but he doesn't need anything else.
And we asked them to-- to use the money for whatever they wanted to buy, television sets or you know, buy some new furniture.
And they just refused.
They said they would put it in-- place it in his account.
We-- we, of course, have no idea what happened to the money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I personally don't care.

But I think it's incredible that, you know, a little country like Belgium, population of 10 million, you know, has-- has the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has the ability and the heart to look after children who come from other places, come from other countries, and treat them like one of their own.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yeah.
In fact, a few years ago, it was interesting.
They-- they take them on trips three or four times a year.
And they take them on shorter trips.
They go on mini trips.
Day trips they do all the time.
But three or four times a year, they take them for five or six days.
They take them to the seashore.
They take them to interesting cities in Europe.
And a few years ago, they were going to Eastern Europe, I believe.
And the-- when we were there, they told us that they wouldn't take him, because they were afraid, because he was Jewish.
So they're very aware of it.
In fact, there is-- I have an incredible story.

A-- a dentist from Antwerp, a very, very Orthodox Jew, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I don't know, maybe 10, 15 years ago, was researching [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some records to find out if anyone needed help from him.
In other words, he was looking to do some good deeds.
I mean, this is very important to the Orthodox, you know.
They have-- they want to do their share of [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, not only by giving money, but by actually physically helping someone who needs help.
And my brother-- they discovered my brother.
And this man lives, as I said, in Antwerp.
It's about a two hour car journey for him.
And since then, he visits him every year.
He can't communicate with him.
I mean, he's got-- simply because he has nothing to say to him.
And it must be very awkward, but lately his wife has also been going.
And they just come and bring him some candies or something and sit with him for an hour or two and then go home.
And my brother loves visits.
Even though he can't really communicate, but he loves the attention.
And I think it's just wonderful.
And we ride up and see him once or twice, maybe two or three times, to this dentist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he's tried to get other-- other people from his community to go, but he hasn't-- he hasn't succeeded.
It's-- it must be extremely difficult to visit someone that you can't really connect to at any level.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it really made me feel good.
Yes, my parents wrote.
I mean, writing was permitted.
Until the war broke out, communication was possible.
I-- I'm sorry?
They couldn't.
These countries would only accept children.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At least they accepted children, something the United States refused to do, except 1,000 children.
It was really non-denominational, although I would say 95% of the children were Catholic.
Belgium was largely a Catholic country.
But we were not managed by nuns.
The people who managed this orphanage were not of any particular denomination, although most were-- were Catholic.
But they did not-- they were not nuns.
Yes, it was a problem.
When I-- of course, I didn't speak a word of French when I went.
And I should have gone into the third grade, and for some reason, I just started the third grade.
For some reason, they decided that since I didn't know French, I should go back to the second grade.
So I was very upset that I lost a year.
But I learned French very quickly.
It-- children learn-- especially if you are immersed in it.
Nobody spoke German.
So French was the only language that I could, in any way, shape or form, communicate in.
And it was not a problem.
Difficult at first.
I had learned to write the German script, which is not-- it's the-- it's a Gothic script, which we were still using in Germany at that time.

And when I came to Belgium, I had never seen the Latin script that we use today, and so I had to teach myself to-- they didn't have ESLs, or they didn't-- or FSLs, or-- There was no special help for me.
And I just had to teach myself to read and write all over again.
To, you know, figure out what the letters stood for and how to-- how to write them into Latin script.
I remember that.
Otherwise, school was not a problem.
I was a very good student.
No, not really.
I-- I mean, there were children I became friendly with.
Were they really close?
No, I don't think so.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- in 1940-- in 1940, I was very sickly, and I lost some weight, I don't know.
And so this orphanage had a place-- had a-- a place where they sent children who needed more sunshine, or, you know, better nourishment.
Not that we were not well nourished.
So I was sent to this place on the north coast of Belgium called Middelkerke.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I left the particular unit that I was associated with.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there for a few months.
Of course, I missed school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] During that time, the war broke out.
And the war with Belgium only lasted 10 days.
The Belgians capitulated in 10 days.
France didn't last much longer.
Nobody, at that time, in 1940, could withstand the onslaught of the Germans.
But this particular unit was closed.
And there were several Jewish children there.
That's where I first met other Jewish children.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were Jewish children in the orphanage.
I didn't know it, but I learned about it afterwards.
And those Jewish children were, as soon as the war broke out, or a little bit after, were sent away.
They were gathered together, and sent to the South of France, which was supposed to be safe.

It was known that once the Germans invaded, that the lives of these children, including myself, I guess, our lives would be very difficult and would pose a danger, not only to ourselves, but to those who kept us.
To harbor a Jewish child, to harbor any Jew, to help any Jew could cost you your life.
And this became a major preoccupation of mine.
I was terrified that I would be discovered and that the whole orphanage might be closed because of me.
I don't know if the others felt the same, but I was absolutely terrified.
In any case, we were sent back to the orphanage.
And this time, I went to a different unit, which was a little upsetting, since I had gotten used to this place.
I mean, this was now my third stop.
First in the first building, and then the-- the place in Middelkerke, where I did meet some Jewish children that I'm still friendly with.
Well, we lost contact for many years, but we did find each other eventually.
And went back and was moved twice.
They-- I guess they were looking for the right spot.
I have no idea.
I came back from Middelkerke, went to a different place, and then went to a different building again.
You can call the building, unit, if you like, where I stayed for the rest of the-- my stay in the orphanage, which lasted almost eight years.
So I grew up there.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Life in the orphanage [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was not bad.
I never thought I had a bad experience in that orphanage.
In terms of being taken care of, they looked after me as well as they looked after any of the children.
There was no difference between a Jewish child and a non-Jewish child.
The Belgians were very anti-German.
They were not anti-Semitic.
Nobody cared what I was Jewish.
They did-- it did bother some that I was German though, which used to infuriate me, because being German was the last thing I wanted to be.

And to be accused of being German, you know, in fights I might have had with other children, which I did have, to be called a-- you know, by some nasty word because I was German, used to, oh, boy, you know, I'd tear the kids' hair out.
We'd come to blows, to physical blows, and had to be-- you know, broken-- had to be taken apart.
But, otherwise, we were taken care of.
We were very hungry during the war.
There was very little food.
But everybody else was hungry, too, so it certainly bothered me, but I never felt discriminated against.
What we lacked, and I didn't know then, and I figured it out much later, was a close relationship.
You know, there was nobody to touch you or hug you or say good night or wish you a happy birthday.
Although we used to celebrate each other's birthdays, you know, between the small group.
But there was no-- most of the children actually had relatives who used to visit them.
We did not.
The Jewish children did not.
So there was one other Jewish child in my unit, and he and I were the only ones who never had any visitors.
So we did feel kind of left out and a little bit envious of the other kids who-- you know, whoever visited them might bring them some candy or might bring them a-- a toy or something.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The visitors were allowed once a week.
And once a month, the children were allowed to go out, to, you know, to leave the orphanage accompanied by an adult relative who would pick them up.
And, of course, on those once a month, then we would be left all alone in-- in this-- in our unit.
We literally were the only ones who didn't have anyone to take us out.
In my unit, there were 60, and we were divided into three different sections.
So there were 20 in my section, but there were really 60 of us.
But one adult took care of 20 children.
So that was not too bad.
It worked very well.
It was-- it was-- it was a very disciplined environment.
Yes.
Yeah, yeah.
And this was somewhat upsetting.
It-- you know, time kind of dragged on when you're all alone, and-- and we knew why we had no one to visit us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, otherwise, I always felt that my experience in this orphanage was a very positive one.
Well, I became very self-sufficient.
I mean, this was all rationalization that came much later.
I became very grown up.
I didn't need anyone to take care of me.
You know, when I was 8, or 9, or 10, I could take care of myself.
I didn't need a mother.
I didn't need a father.
I wasn't dependent on anybody else.
I was only dependent on myself, and I thought that was a real plus.
I continued to think that for many, many years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I've changed my mind since, but it-- it made it possible to, you know, to survive.
From my mother-- I don't recall ever getting a letter from my father-- my mother wrote.
She-- it would just be short, chatty letters.
Unfortunately, I somehow, I moved a lot in my life.
I lost them.
I don't know what happened to them.
I don't have them any more.
I kept them for a long, long time.
And in one of my moves, I must have-- I must have fallen through the cracks, I don't know.
Well, I knew that my father had left for the United States in-- in May of 1940.
I knew that my mother was left all alone with my grandmothers.
Eventually, my grandmothers did leave for South America.
Both grandmothers went to South America.
They couldn't-- they would have liked to come to the United States, but that was never an option for them.
They could not get visas.
I had two uncles and aunts.
One my father's brother, one my mother's brother, who also were waiting for that magic visa to the United States which never materialized, and they perished in the Holocaust.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother miraculously-- she wrote to me until the summer of 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I got a last letter from her saying that I wouldn't be hearing any-- anymore.
And I-- I knew why.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SOB] I'm usually better-- [CHUCKLES]
My mother-- when?
Yes, when the letters stopped.
Yeah.
Well, she told me she was going away.
And that I wouldn't be hearing for a while.
And I knew what was going on.
I-- I don't know how I knew.
Nobody told me anything, but I must have heard something about what's going on in Germany.
I must have-- who knows where.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She was arrested and sent away, sent to concentration camp.
Well, the first-- the first two years, more or less, she was in labor camps in Estonia, Lithuania, with one of the younger members of her group was the girl who made that sculpture back there, Ilsa.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eventually, she ended up in Stutthof.
The concentration camp, Stutthof.
It's a small-- it was a small camp and all-- a very terrible one with-- and she-- there she became really frightened that she would be-- it had a gas chamber next to it.
It was an extermination camp where, luckily, this was in late '44, and she was liberated, well, days, went on the death march in January of 1945, I believe.
And then the Russians-- and then the SS disappeared one day.
And they were liberated.
They were free.
Of course, I didn't know any of this at that time.
Yes, after the war.
When the war ended in 19-- in Belgium, the war ended in September of 1944, and the Red Cross got in touch with me.
I guess my father must have asked the Red Cross to find out where I was, and-- which they did.
And so I started communicating with my father.
No, he was actually living in-- in Bridgeport, Connecticut.
He had come in 1940.
He had a brother here who at that time, I believe, lived in Newark.
So he came to Newark, but eventually went to Bridgeport, where there were a lot of jobs.
Bridgeport was a very industrial town.
There were many factories there.
And he got a job in one of the factories.
And he did not do very well, but-- I mean, financially, he didn't do very well, but at least he was safe.
And, of course, he didn't know what happened to his family.
It must have been very difficult for him.
Oh, that was--
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
I'll never forget it.
It was very exhilarating.
I think I mentioned how frightened I was that-- I guess I didn't tell you that after 1942, the few Jewish children in the orphanage could no longer attend school.
It was too dangerous.
We had to wear the star, and it was just too dangerous to go out.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yeah, we did not have Russian cards.
It didn't really matter.

There were only, as far as I knew, six or seven Jewish children in the orphanage, and out of, you know, 350 or so, it didn't make a great impact on the amount of food that we received, which was very small.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no one ever mentioned it.
But we knew.
But going to school, leaving-- leaving the confines of the orphanage became very dangerous, and we were no longer allowed to do that.
But the-- the orphanage, the grounds were very spacious.
We were allowed to wander around the grounds.
Not that we did that, because we didn't see much point.
We were kept busy.
I mean, if you live with 60 other children, there's always a lot to do.
And so we were kept busy doing, basically, chores.
Our education stopped altogether.
We were advised to do homework with the other children, which was really not very productive.
I never did.
I just didn't find that a way of learning.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And books were not readily available.
I think I read maybe two or three books during those years, because we didn't have books.
We did-- we were not given the opportunity to go to libraries.
We didn't have-- I'm not sure that libraries existed.
I'm sure they existed in a town, but the orphanage was not really focused on education.
It was focused on, at least, feeding and housing children for a while.
Very few were there as long as I was.
That was another problem.
You tried not to get too attached to anybody because you knew they would leave.
And so it was dangerous.
Again, for me, it was another sign that I had to depend only on myself.
I could not form a friendship or an attachment, since the attachment would, again, you know, be-- be, in the end, a negative experience for me.
Well, they-- yeah, they-- I was told not to use my name, you know, which didn't sound Jewish, but it sounded German.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I used my middle name, Margaret.
So everybody was told to call me Margaret, and somehow nobody really asked why.
It didn't seem to be a problem.
I did not have the kind of problems that someone who passed as a Christian had.
You know, the-- the-- who had to go to church, and know all the catechisms, and know all-- know all the prayers and the signs and the crossing yourself at appropriate times.
No, there was no anti-Semitism.
No, I never knew-- to this day, I don't know why we were kept in hiding, but yet, not literally hidden.
Since these children had visitors from the outside, I can't believe that these outside visitors were not aware of my existence.
Maybe they weren't.
